title: Vitamin A
content: Vitamin A (also known as Retinol) is a vitamin. Key benefits: Supports vision (especially night vision); immune function; skin health; cell growth. Food sources: Liver, egg yolks, dairy, carrots, sweet potatoes, spinach, kale, mango. Adult RDA: 900 mcg RAE (men) / 700 mcg RAE (women). Deficiency symptoms: Night blindness, dry eyes, dry skin, frequent infections. Deficiency disease(s): Night blindness / Xerophthalmia.

title: Vitamin B1
content: Vitamin B1 (also known as Thiamin) is a vitamin. Key benefits: Helps convert food into energy; supports nerve and muscle function. Food sources: Whole grains, pork, legumes, seeds, nuts, fortified cereals. Adult RDA: 1.2 mg (men) / 1.1 mg (women). Deficiency symptoms: Fatigue, irritability, nerve damage, muscle weakness. Deficiency disease(s): Beriberi.

title: Vitamin B2
content: Vitamin B2 (also known as Riboflavin) is a vitamin. Key benefits: Supports energy production; cell function; growth; helps break down fats and drugs. Food sources: Dairy, eggs, lean meats, green vegetables, fortified grains. Adult RDA: 1.3 mg (men) / 1.1 mg (women). Deficiency symptoms: Cracked lips, sore throat, skin disorders, eye fatigue. Deficiency disease(s): Ariboflavinosis.

title: Vitamin B3
content: Vitamin B3 (also known as Niacin) is a vitamin. Key benefits: Supports digestive system; skin; nerves; helps convert food to energy. Food sources: Meat, fish, poultry, whole grains, peanuts, legumes. Adult RDA: 16 mg (men) / 14 mg (women). Deficiency symptoms: Fatigue, skin rashes, digestive issues, mental confusion. Deficiency disease(s): Pellagra.

title: Vitamin B5
content: Vitamin B5 (also known as Pantothenic Acid) is a vitamin. Key benefits: Helps synthesize coenzyme A for fatty acid metabolism; supports hormone production. Food sources: Chicken, beef, potatoes, oats, tomatoes, whole grains, mushrooms. Adult RDA: 5 mg. Deficiency symptoms: Fatigue, irritability, numbness, tingling in hands/feet. Deficiency disease(s): Rare - Paresthesia.

title: Vitamin B6
content: Vitamin B6 (also known as Pyridoxine) is a vitamin. Key benefits: Supports brain development; immune function; amino acid metabolism. Food sources: Poultry, fish, potatoes, chickpeas, bananas, fortified cereals. Adult RDA: 1.3 mg. Deficiency symptoms: Anemia, skin rashes, confusion, weakened immunity. Deficiency disease(s): Peripheral neuropathy.

title: Vitamin B7
content: Vitamin B7 (also known as Biotin) is a vitamin. Key benefits: Supports metabolism of fats; carbohydrates; proteins; supports hair and nail health. Food sources: Eggs, nuts, seeds, salmon, sweet potatoes, organ meats. Adult RDA: 30 mcg. Deficiency symptoms: Hair thinning, brittle nails, skin rashes. Deficiency disease(s): Biotin deficiency (rare).

title: Vitamin B9
content: Vitamin B9 (also known as Folate / Folic Acid) is a vitamin. Key benefits: Essential for DNA synthesis and cell division; critical during pregnancy for fetal development. Food sources: Leafy greens, legumes, citrus fruits, asparagus, fortified grains. Adult RDA: 400 mcg DFE. Deficiency symptoms: Fatigue, weakness, poor concentration, birth defects if deficient in pregnancy. Deficiency disease(s): Folate-deficiency anemia.
keywords: Vitamin B9; Folate / Folic Acid; Vitamin

title: Vitamin B12
content: Vitamin B12 (also known as Cobalamin) is a vitamin. Key benefits: Supports red blood cell formation; neurological function; DNA synthesis. Food sources: Meat, fish, dairy, eggs, fortified nutritional yeast (not found in plant foods). Adult RDA: 2.4 mcg. Deficiency symptoms: Fatigue, weakness, numbness/tingling, memory problems, pale skin. Deficiency disease(s): Pernicious anemia / B12-deficiency anemia.

title: Vitamin C
content: Vitamin C (also known as Ascorbic Acid) is a vitamin. Key benefits: Antioxidant; supports immune function; collagen synthesis; iron absorption; wound healing. Food sources: Citrus fruits, strawberries, bell peppers, broccoli, tomatoes, kiwi. Adult RDA: 90 mg (men) / 75 mg (women). Deficiency symptoms: Bleeding gums, slow wound healing, joint pain, easy bruising. Deficiency disease(s): Scurvy.

title: Vitamin D
content: Vitamin D (also known as Calciferol) is a vitamin. Key benefits: Supports calcium absorption; bone health; immune and muscle function. Food sources: Fatty fish, egg yolks, fortified milk, mushrooms; also produced via sun exposure. Adult RDA: 600-800 IU (15-20 mcg). Deficiency symptoms: Bone pain, muscle weakness, increased fracture risk. Deficiency disease(s): Rickets (children) / Osteomalacia (adults).

title: Vitamin E
content: Vitamin E (also known as Tocopherol) is a vitamin. Key benefits: Antioxidant; protects cells from damage; supports immune function and skin health. Food sources: Nuts, seeds, vegetable oils, spinach, avocado. Adult RDA: 15 mg. Deficiency symptoms: Muscle weakness, vision problems, immune issues (rare, usually from fat malabsorption). Deficiency disease(s): Vitamin E deficiency (rare).

title: Vitamin K
content: Vitamin K (also known as Phylloquinone / Menaquinone) is a vitamin. Key benefits: Essential for blood clotting and bone metabolism. Food sources: Leafy greens (kale, spinach), broccoli, Brussels sprouts, fermented foods. Adult RDA: 120 mcg (men) / 90 mcg (women). Deficiency symptoms: Easy bruising, excessive bleeding, poor bone health. Deficiency disease(s): Vitamin K deficiency bleeding.

title: Calcium
content: Calcium is a mineral. Key benefits: Builds and maintains bones and teeth; supports muscle function; nerve signaling; blood clotting. Food sources: Dairy products, leafy greens, fortified plant milks, tofu, almonds, sardines. Adult RDA: 1000-1200 mg. Deficiency symptoms: Muscle cramps, brittle nails, numbness, increased fracture risk. Deficiency disease(s): Osteoporosis / Osteopenia.

title: Iron
content: Iron is a mineral. Key benefits: Essential for hemoglobin production and oxygen transport in the blood. Food sources: Red meat, poultry, seafood, lentils, beans, spinach, fortified cereals. Adult RDA: 8 mg (men) / 18 mg (women). Deficiency symptoms: Fatigue, pale skin, weakness, shortness of breath, cold hands/feet. Deficiency disease(s): Iron-deficiency anemia.

title: Magnesium
content: Magnesium is a mineral. Key benefits: Supports muscle and nerve function; blood sugar regulation; blood pressure; bone health. Food sources: Nuts, seeds, whole grains, leafy greens, legumes, dark chocolate. Adult RDA: 400-420 mg (men) / 310-320 mg (women). Deficiency symptoms: Muscle cramps, fatigue, irregular heartbeat, numbness. Deficiency disease(s): Hypomagnesemia.

title: Zinc
content: Zinc is a mineral. Key benefits: Supports immune function; wound healing; DNA synthesis; taste/smell. Food sources: Meat, shellfish, legumes, seeds, nuts, dairy. Adult RDA: 11 mg (men) / 8 mg (women). Deficiency symptoms: Hair loss, delayed wound healing, weakened immunity, loss of appetite. Deficiency disease(s): Zinc deficiency.

title: Potassium
content: Potassium is a mineral. Key benefits: Regulates fluid balance; muscle contractions; nerve signals; supports heart function. Food sources: Bananas, potatoes, beans, spinach, avocado, oranges. Adult RDA: 2600-3400 mg. Deficiency symptoms: Muscle weakness, cramps, irregular heartbeat, fatigue. Deficiency disease(s): Hypokalemia.

title: Sodium
content: Sodium is a mineral. Key benefits: Regulates fluid balance and blood pressure; supports nerve and muscle function. Food sources: Table salt, processed foods, bread, cheese, canned soups. Adult RDA: 1500-2300 mg (upper limit). Deficiency symptoms: Nausea, muscle cramps, confusion, low blood pressure (deficiency is rare). Deficiency disease(s): Hyponatremia.

title: Phosphorus
content: Phosphorus is a mineral. Key benefits: Works with calcium to build bones and teeth; supports energy storage and cell membrane structure. Food sources: Meat, dairy, fish, nuts, legumes, whole grains. Adult RDA: 700 mg. Deficiency symptoms: Bone pain, weakness, loss of appetite (deficiency is rare). Deficiency disease(s): Hypophosphatemia.

title: Iodine
content: Iodine is a mineral. Key benefits: Essential for thyroid hormone production; which regulates metabolism. Food sources: Iodized salt, seaweed, fish, dairy, eggs. Adult RDA: 150 mcg. Deficiency symptoms: Goiter, fatigue, weight gain, developmental delays in infants. Deficiency disease(s): Iodine-deficiency disorder / Goiter.

title: Selenium
content: Selenium is a mineral. Key benefits: Antioxidant; supports thyroid function and immune health. Food sources: Brazil nuts, seafood, meat, eggs, sunflower seeds. Adult RDA: 55 mcg. Deficiency symptoms: Muscle weakness, fatigue, weakened immunity, thyroid problems. Deficiency disease(s): Keshan disease (rare).

title: Copper
content: Copper is a mineral. Key benefits: Supports iron metabolism; connective tissue formation; nervous system function. Food sources: Shellfish, nuts, seeds, whole grains, organ meats, dark chocolate. Adult RDA: 900 mcg. Deficiency symptoms: Fatigue, pale skin, weakened immunity, brittle bones. Deficiency disease(s): Copper deficiency (rare).

title: Manganese
content: Manganese is a mineral. Key benefits: Supports bone formation; metabolism; antioxidant defense. Food sources: Whole grains, nuts, legumes, tea, leafy vegetables. Adult RDA: 2.3 mg (men) / 1.8 mg (women). Deficiency symptoms: Bone malformation, weakness, impaired growth (rare). Deficiency disease(s): Manganese deficiency (rare).

title: Chromium
content: Chromium is a mineral. Key benefits: Enhances insulin action and supports carbohydrate/fat metabolism. Food sources: Broccoli, whole grains, meat, nuts, brewer's yeast. Adult RDA: 35 mcg (men) / 25 mcg (women). Deficiency symptoms: Impaired glucose tolerance, unintended weight loss (rare). Deficiency disease(s): Chromium deficiency (rare).

title: Fluoride
content: Fluoride is a mineral. Key benefits: Strengthens tooth enamel and helps prevent tooth decay. Food sources: Fluoridated water, tea, seafood, fluoride toothpaste. Adult RDA: 3-4 mg. Deficiency symptoms: Increased risk of tooth decay. Deficiency disease(s): Dental caries risk.

title: Innate vs Adaptive Immunity
content: The immune system has two main branches. Innate immunity is the body's fast, non-specific first line of defense, active from birth, including physical barriers (skin, mucus), and cells like neutrophils and macrophages that attack any invader. Adaptive immunity is slower to activate but highly specific and creates memory of past pathogens, driven mainly by T-cells and B-cells.
keywords: innate immunity; adaptive immunity; immune system overview; first line of defense

title: Helper T-cells (CD4+)
content: Helper T-cells (CD4+ T-cells) coordinate the immune response by releasing cytokines that activate other immune cells, including B-cells and cytotoxic T-cells. They do not kill infected cells directly but act as the 'command center' of adaptive immunity. HIV specifically targets and depletes CD4+ helper T-cells, which is why it severely weakens immune function.
keywords: helper T cells; CD4; cytokines; adaptive immunity; T lymphocytes

title: Cytotoxic T-cells (CD8+)
content: Cytotoxic T-cells (CD8+ T-cells) directly kill cells that are infected with viruses or that have become cancerous. They recognize infected cells via antigen fragments displayed on the cell surface (MHC class I molecules) and release enzymes that induce cell death in the target cell.
keywords: cytotoxic T cells; CD8; killer T cells; MHC class I; viral infection

title: Regulatory T-cells (Tregs)
content: Regulatory T-cells (Tregs) suppress and control the immune response to prevent it from attacking the body's own tissues, and to shut the response down once an infection is cleared. Dysfunction in Tregs is linked to autoimmune diseases.
keywords: regulatory T cells; Tregs; immune tolerance; autoimmune disease

title: B-cells and Antibody Production
content: B-cells are white blood cells responsible for producing antibodies (immunoglobulins). When a B-cell encounters an antigen it recognizes, with help from helper T-cells it differentiates into plasma cells that mass-produce antibodies specific to that antigen, and memory B-cells that allow a faster response if the same pathogen returns.
keywords: B cells; antibodies; immunoglobulins; plasma cells; memory B cells

title: Types of Antibodies
content: There are five main antibody classes: IgG (most abundant, provides long-term immunity and crosses the placenta), IgA (found in mucosal secretions like saliva and breast milk), IgM (first antibody produced during an infection), IgE (involved in allergic reactions and parasite defense), and IgD (function still not fully understood, found on B-cell surfaces).
keywords: IgG; IgA; IgM; IgE; IgD; antibody classes; immunoglobulin types

title: Types of White Blood Cells (Leukocytes)
content: White blood cells are grouped into granulocytes and agranulocytes. Granulocytes include neutrophils (most abundant, first responders that engulf bacteria), eosinophils (fight parasites and involved in allergic responses), and basophils (release histamine during allergic reactions). Agranulocytes include lymphocytes (T-cells, B-cells, natural killer cells) and monocytes, which mature into macrophages that engulf pathogens and debris.
keywords: white blood cells; leukocytes; neutrophils; eosinophils; basophils; monocytes; macrophages

title: Primary vs Secondary Immune Response
content: The primary immune response occurs the first time the body encounters a pathogen and takes days to build up, while producing memory cells. The secondary immune response occurs on re-exposure to the same pathogen; because memory B-cells and T-cells already exist, the response is much faster and stronger. Vaccines work by safely triggering a primary response so the body is ready with a fast secondary response if it meets the real pathogen later.
keywords: primary immune response; secondary immune response; immunological memory; vaccination

title: How Nutrition Supports Immune Function
content: Several nutrients are directly required for immune cell production and function: Vitamin C supports white blood cell function and acts as an antioxidant; Vitamin D modulates both innate and adaptive immune responses; zinc is essential for the development and function of neutrophils and natural killer cells; and protein provides the building blocks for antibodies. Chronic deficiency in any of these can impair immune response and increase infection risk.
keywords: nutrition and immunity; vitamin C immunity; vitamin D immunity; zinc immunity; protein immunity

title: Vegetarian Diet Overview
content: A vegetarian diet excludes meat, poultry, and fish but typically includes dairy and eggs (lacto-ovo vegetarian). It emphasizes vegetables, fruits, whole grains, legumes, nuts, and seeds. Key nutritional considerations include ensuring adequate iron, vitamin B12 (mainly from dairy/eggs), zinc, and complete protein through combining grains and legumes.
keywords: vegetarian diet; lacto-ovo vegetarian; plant-based; iron; vitamin B12

title: Non-Vegetarian Diet Overview
content: A non-vegetarian diet includes meat, poultry, fish, and seafood alongside plant foods. It naturally provides complete protein, heme iron (more easily absorbed than plant-based iron), vitamin B12, and omega-3 fatty acids from fish. Balance and moderation matter: excessive red or processed meat intake is associated with higher cardiovascular risk, so lean meats, poultry, and fish are generally favored over processed meats.
keywords: non-vegetarian diet; heme iron; complete protein; omega-3; lean meat

title: Ketogenic (Keto) Diet
content: The ketogenic diet is a very low-carbohydrate, high-fat, moderate-protein diet (typically around 5-10% carbs, 70-75% fat, 15-20% protein) designed to shift the body into ketosis, where it burns fat for fuel instead of glucose. It was originally developed to manage epilepsy and is also used for weight loss. Common foods include meat, fatty fish, eggs, butter, nuts, and low-carb vegetables, while grains, sugar, and most fruits are avoided. It requires medical supervision for people with diabetes or kidney conditions.
keywords: ketogenic diet; keto; low carb; ketosis; high fat diet

title: Mediterranean Diet
content: The Mediterranean diet is based on traditional eating patterns of countries bordering the Mediterranean Sea. It emphasizes olive oil as the primary fat source, vegetables, fruits, whole grains, legumes, nuts, and fish, with moderate dairy and poultry and limited red meat. It is widely studied and associated with reduced risk of cardiovascular disease, largely due to its high content of monounsaturated fats, fiber, and antioxidants.
keywords: mediterranean diet; olive oil; heart health; monounsaturated fat; fiber

title: Immunity-Boosting Diet Principles
content: An immunity-supportive diet focuses on nutrient density rather than any single 'superfood.' Key priorities include: adequate protein for antibody production; vitamin C-rich foods (citrus, bell peppers, broccoli); vitamin D (fatty fish, fortified foods, sun exposure); zinc (seeds, legumes, meat); probiotic/fermented foods (yogurt, kefir) for gut-immune health; and limiting added sugar and excessive alcohol, both of which can suppress immune cell function.
keywords: immunity boosting diet; immune supportive foods; gut health; probiotics; nutrient density

title: Vegan Diet Overview
content: A vegan diet excludes all animal products, including dairy, eggs, and honey, relying entirely on plant foods. It requires deliberate planning to avoid deficiencies in vitamin B12 (not naturally present in plant foods and typically requires fortified foods or supplementation), iron, calcium, omega-3 fatty acids, and sometimes protein, depending on food variety and intake.
keywords: vegan diet; plant-based; vitamin B12 supplementation; calcium; omega-3 ALA

title: Scurvy
content: Scurvy is caused by prolonged vitamin C (ascorbic acid) deficiency. Vitamin C is required to synthesize collagen, so deficiency weakens connective tissue, leading to bleeding gums, easy bruising, joint pain, poor wound healing, and fatigue. It is treated and prevented with adequate intake of citrus fruits, bell peppers, and other vitamin C-rich foods.
keywords: scurvy; vitamin C deficiency; collagen; bleeding gums; ascorbic acid

title: Anemia
content: Anemia is a condition where the blood lacks enough healthy red blood cells or hemoglobin to carry adequate oxygen. Nutritional anemia is most commonly caused by iron deficiency (iron-deficiency anemia), but can also result from vitamin B12 deficiency (pernicious/megaloblastic anemia) or folate deficiency. Symptoms include fatigue, pale skin, shortness of breath, and dizziness.
keywords: anemia; iron deficiency anemia; B12 deficiency; folate deficiency; hemoglobin

title: Rickets
content: Rickets is a childhood disease caused by vitamin D, calcium, or phosphate deficiency, resulting in softening and weakening of bones. It leads to skeletal deformities such as bowed legs, delayed growth, and bone pain. The adult equivalent, caused by the same deficiencies, is called osteomalacia. Prevention includes adequate vitamin D from sunlight, fortified foods, and fatty fish.
keywords: rickets; vitamin D deficiency; osteomalacia; bone softening; calcium

title: Beriberi
content: Beriberi is caused by thiamine (vitamin B1) deficiency. 'Wet beriberi' affects the cardiovascular system, causing swelling and heart failure, while 'dry beriberi' affects the nervous system, causing muscle weakness, nerve damage, and difficulty walking. It is historically linked to diets based heavily on polished (white) rice with little else.
keywords: beriberi; thiamine deficiency; vitamin B1; wet beriberi; dry beriberi

title: Pellagra
content: Pellagra is caused by niacin (vitamin B3) deficiency, often associated with diets heavily reliant on untreated corn/maize. It is classically described by the '4 Ds': dermatitis (skin rash on sun-exposed areas), diarrhea, dementia, and if untreated, death. Treatment involves niacin supplementation and dietary diversification.
keywords: pellagra; niacin deficiency; vitamin B3; dermatitis; four Ds

title: Goiter and Iodine Deficiency Disorders
content: Iodine deficiency impairs the thyroid gland's ability to produce thyroid hormone, causing the gland to enlarge (goiter) as it tries to compensate. Severe deficiency during pregnancy can cause irreversible cognitive impairment in the child (cretinism). Iodized salt and seafood are the main dietary sources used to prevent it.
keywords: iodine deficiency; goiter; thyroid; cretinism; iodized salt

title: Kwashiorkor and Marasmus
content: Kwashiorkor and marasmus are severe forms of protein-energy malnutrition, most common in children in food-insecure regions. Kwashiorkor results primarily from protein deficiency despite adequate calorie intake, causing edema (swelling), a distended belly, and fatty liver. Marasmus results from severe overall calorie and nutrient deficiency, causing extreme wasting of muscle and fat with no edema.
keywords: kwashiorkor; marasmus; protein energy malnutrition; edema; child malnutrition

title: Night Blindness / Xerophthalmia
content: Vitamin A deficiency impairs the retina's ability to adapt to low light, causing night blindness, and can progress to xerophthalmia (severe dryness of the eyes), corneal damage, and in advanced untreated cases, irreversible blindness. It remains a leading cause of preventable childhood blindness in developing countries.
keywords: vitamin A deficiency; night blindness; xerophthalmia; retinol; childhood blindness

title: Turmeric and Curcumin
content: Turmeric is a spice containing curcumin, a compound with notable anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. Research suggests curcumin may help reduce markers of inflammation and support joint health, though curcumin has poor bioavailability on its own and is often paired with black pepper (piperine) to improve absorption. It is commonly used in cooking and as a supplement.
keywords: turmeric; curcumin; anti-inflammatory; antioxidant; piperine; bioavailability

title: Ginger
content: Ginger is a root widely used for its anti-nausea and digestive benefits, including relief from motion sickness and pregnancy-related nausea. It contains gingerol, a bioactive compound with anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects, and is also commonly used to help ease muscle soreness and support digestion.
keywords: ginger; gingerol; nausea relief; digestion; anti-inflammatory

title: Garlic
content: Garlic contains allicin, a sulfur compound formed when garlic is crushed or chopped, which is associated with cardiovascular benefits such as modestly lower blood pressure and cholesterol, as well as antimicrobial properties. Regular consumption is also studied for potential immune-supportive effects, though effect sizes in human studies vary.
keywords: garlic; allicin; cardiovascular health; antimicrobial; cholesterol

title: Green Tea
content: Green tea is rich in catechins, particularly EGCG (epigallocatechin gallate), a powerful antioxidant. It is associated with modest benefits for metabolism, cardiovascular health, and cellular protection against oxidative stress. It also contains a moderate amount of caffeine along with L-theanine, which together are thought to produce a calmer form of alertness compared to coffee.
keywords: green tea; EGCG; catechins; antioxidant; L-theanine

title: Berries (Blueberries, Strawberries)
content: Berries are dense in anthocyanins and other polyphenol antioxidants, which help combat oxidative stress linked to aging and chronic disease. They are also a good source of fiber and vitamin C relative to their calorie content, and are commonly associated with cognitive and cardiovascular health benefits in observational studies.

title: Yogurt and Fermented Foods
content: Fermented foods like yogurt, kefir, and kimchi contain live probiotic bacteria that support gut microbiome diversity. Since a large portion of the immune system is closely tied to gut health, regular intake of fermented foods is associated with improved digestion and may support immune function indirectly through the gut-immune axis.
keywords: yogurt; probiotics; fermented foods; gut microbiome; gut-immune axis

title: High-Protein Diet Plan
content: A high-protein diet emphasizes protein at every meal to support muscle building, recovery, satiety, and weight management. Target roughly 1.2-2.0 g of protein per kg of body weight depending on activity. Best protein sources: chicken breast, fish, eggs, Greek yogurt, lentils, chickpeas, tofu, and whey. Sample day: eggs and Greek yogurt at breakfast; grilled chicken or lentil bowl at lunch; fish or tofu with vegetables at dinner; nuts or cottage cheese as snacks. Pair protein with fibre-rich vegetables and whole grains for balance. Good for: muscle gain, active individuals, satiety during weight loss. Keep saturated fat moderate by favouring lean and plant proteins.
keywords: high protein; muscle building; protein rich; lean protein; chicken; fish; eggs; lentils; recovery; satiety

title: Low-Fat Diet Plan
content: A low-fat diet limits total and saturated fat to support heart health, weight management, and conditions needing reduced fat intake. Keep fat to roughly 20-30% of calories and minimize fried and processed foods. Emphasize: vegetables, fruits, whole grains, legumes, lean poultry, white fish, and non-fat dairy. Cook by baking, steaming, grilling, or boiling rather than frying. Sample day: oats with fruit; a large salad with grilled chicken and light dressing; steamed fish with vegetables and brown rice. Use healthy fats (olive oil, nuts) in small controlled amounts rather than eliminating them entirely. Good for: heart health, high cholesterol, gallbladder issues, calorie control.
keywords: low fat; heart healthy; reduced fat; lean; low cholesterol diet; baked; steamed; weight management

title: Low-Carb Diet Plan
content: A low-carb diet reduces carbohydrate intake to help control blood sugar, support weight loss, and steady energy. Limit refined carbs (white bread, sugar, sweets) and moderate starchy foods; get carbs mainly from vegetables and some fruit. Emphasize: protein, non-starchy vegetables, healthy fats, eggs, fish, poultry, nuts, and seeds. Sample day: eggs and avocado; a chicken and vegetable salad; salmon with greens; nuts or cheese as snacks. Especially useful for people managing diabetes or insulin resistance because it blunts blood-sugar spikes. Good for: type 2 diabetes, weight loss, blood-sugar control. Ensure enough fibre from vegetables to aid digestion.
keywords: low carb; blood sugar control; diabetes diet; weight loss; reduced carbohydrate; insulin resistance; keto adjacent

title: Low-Sodium Diet Plan
content: A low-sodium diet limits salt to support healthy blood pressure and reduce strain on the heart and kidneys. Aim for under about 1,500-2,300 mg of sodium per day. Avoid processed foods, canned soups, cured meats, salty snacks, and excess restaurant food. Emphasize: fresh vegetables and fruit, whole grains, unsalted legumes, fresh lean protein, and herbs and spices instead of salt for flavour. Sample day: oatmeal with fruit; a fresh salad with unsalted grilled chicken; home-cooked fish with steamed vegetables seasoned with lemon and herbs. Read labels for hidden sodium. Good for: high blood pressure (hypertension), heart disease, kidney disease.
keywords: low sodium; low salt; blood pressure; hypertension; heart disease; kidney; DASH; fresh foods

title: High-Fibre Diet Plan
content: A high-fibre diet increases dietary fibre to support digestion, gut health, steady blood sugar, and cholesterol control. Aim for roughly 25-38 g of fibre per day from whole foods. Emphasize: whole grains (oats, brown rice, whole wheat), legumes (lentils, beans, chickpeas), vegetables, fruit with skin, nuts, and seeds. Increase fibre gradually and drink plenty of water to avoid discomfort. Sample day: oats with berries and chia; a lentil and vegetable bowl; brown rice with beans and greens; fruit and nuts as snacks. Good for: constipation, gut health, high cholesterol, blood-sugar stability, and satiety during weight management.
keywords: high fibre; high fiber; digestion; gut health; whole grains; legumes; cholesterol; constipation; satiety

title: Balanced Diet Plan
content: A balanced diet provides all major nutrients in healthy proportions to maintain overall health, energy, and a healthy weight. Roughly fill half the plate with vegetables and fruit, a quarter with lean protein, and a quarter with whole grains, plus some healthy fats and dairy or alternatives. Emphasize variety, whole and minimally processed foods, and appropriate portions. Sample day: whole-grain breakfast with fruit and yogurt; a mixed plate of lean protein, vegetables, and whole grains at lunch and dinner; fruit, nuts, or yogurt as snacks. Good for: general health maintenance, people without a specific clinical restriction, and building sustainable eating habits.

title: Hemoglobin
content: Hemoglobin (also spelled Haemoglobin) is the iron-containing protein in red blood cells that carries oxygen from the lungs to the rest of the body. Normal levels are roughly 13.5-17.5 g/dL for men and 12.0-15.5 g/dL for women. Low hemoglobin (haemoglobin) causes anemia, leading to fatigue, weakness, pale skin, and shortness of breath. Producing hemoglobin requires adequate iron, vitamin B12, and folate. Foods that help raise hemoglobin: iron-rich foods (spinach, lentils, red meat, beans), vitamin C (improves iron absorption), vitamin B12 and folate sources (eggs, dairy, leafy greens).
keywords: Hemoglobin, Haemoglobin, HGB, anemia, iron, oxygen, red blood cells, blood test

title: Chronic Kidney Disease
content: Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD) is the gradual loss of kidney function over time, most commonly caused by diabetes and high blood pressure. Symptoms often appear late and include fatigue, swelling in the legs/ankles, changes in urination, and nausea. CKD is staged 1-5 based on estimated glomerular filtration rate (GFR), with stage 5 being kidney failure. Dietary management for CKD typically involves limiting sodium, potassium, and phosphorus intake, moderating protein intake, and staying within fluid limits as advised by a doctor. Regular blood tests (creatinine, BUN, GFR) are used to monitor kidney health.
keywords: Chronic Kidney Disease, CKD, kidney failure, renal, GFR, creatinine, dialysis, diabetes, blood pressure

title: Vitamin Deficiency Overview
content: Vitamin deficiency occurs when the body doesn't get or absorb enough of a specific vitamin, leading to health problems that range from mild to severe. Common deficiency diseases include Scurvy (vitamin C), Rickets/Osteomalacia (vitamin D), Night Blindness (vitamin A), and Beriberi (vitamin B1). Anemia can result from deficiencies in iron, vitamin B12, or folate. Common causes include poor diet, malabsorption disorders, restrictive diets, and certain medications. General prevention includes eating a varied, balanced diet rich in fruits, vegetables, whole grains, and adequate protein, with supplementation recommended by a doctor when a specific deficiency is diagnosed via blood tests.
keywords: Vitamin deficiency, malnutrition, Scurvy, Rickets, Night Blindness, Beriberi, Anemia, deficiency disease
